html:
CIBL, Inc. (CIBY) Thesis

CIBY is not the $2 microcap described in stale drafts. It is a 10,887-share, $1,705-per-share OTC holding company with a market cap of about $18.6 million. The setup is unusually clean: CIBL reported $19.0 million of cash and liquid investments, or $1,745 per share, at March 31, 2026, with only $655,000 of total liabilities and no debt. In other words, the stock trades roughly at net liquid value while the market assigns almost no value to the operating broadband assets, the 20% Brick Skirt stake, and the Solix stake.

The current story is a quiet asset conversion, not a telecom turnaround. CIBL owns Bretton Woods Telephone and World Surfer in northern New Hampshire, a 20% interest in Brick Skirt in western New York, 10,000 shares of Solix, and a liquid investment portfolio. Q1 2026 revenue rose 20.4% to $632,000 and operating EBITDA rose 54.4% to $193,000, helped by a one-time project, while full-year 2025 revenue rose 15.2% to $2.275 million. The NH business also completed 2 GB broadband availability across Bretton Woods and fiber to the summit of Mount Washington, which should improve the revenue base even if Q1 was not fully repeatable.

The variant perception is that CIBY screens badly but values well. On trailing EPS it looks absurd at roughly 75x to 80x earnings. That is the wrong lens. Corporate overhead consumes a lot of reported income, and investment income moves around with Treasury yields and securities marks. The useful lens is: what am I paying above net cash for a real rural fiber/broadband operation and affiliate stakes? The answer is roughly nothing. Using the company’s March 31 numbers, market cap of $18.6 million versus $19.0 million of liquid assets and $0.7 million of liabilities implies the stock market is valuing the non-liquid operating and affiliate assets at only about $0.2 million.

The buyback is the torque. CIBL repurchased 1,022 shares in 2025 at an average $1,695, then another 357 shares in Q1 2026 at $1,714, retiring 3.2% of the company in one quarter. The board also increased the repurchase authorization, leaving 617 shares authorized at March 31, 2026. For a company with fewer than 11,000 shares outstanding, that remaining authorization is meaningful. Every buyback near liquid value and below book value concentrates the operating assets and affiliate stakes into fewer shares.

This is also a governance and capital allocation story. Mario Gabelli chairs the company, CIBL came out of LICT, and the asset mix is full of related ecosystem holdings. That complexity is why the market ignores it. But the recent additions are interesting: Christopher Nossokoff joined as M&A advisor in 2025, and Gary Sugarman, with telecom/private equity and LICT/MachTen experience, joined the board. That does not guarantee a transaction, but it supports the idea that CIBL is not just passively sitting on T-bills forever.

The retail context is silence. The nominal share price is too high for penny-stock traders, the OTC venue filters out most institutions, and reported volume is only a few shares on many days. That is a feature for the thesis, not a flaw, as long as position size respects the illiquidity. The stock is not being promoted, discovered, or momentum-owned. It is a small ignored cash box slowly shrinking its share count.

Downside is not zero. Management could make a mediocre acquisition, related-party complexity could keep the discount permanent, corporate overhead could continue to absorb operating value, and an impatient seller could move the quote sharply because liquidity is nearly nonexistent. The biggest risk is not business failure. It is value remaining trapped.

But the forward setup is asymmetric. A buyer at $1,705 is effectively paying around net liquid value for a debt-free company with a functioning broadband asset, a western New York broadband affiliate stake, and an active repurchase program. If the company simply keeps buying shares and avoids bad capital allocation, per-share value should grind higher. If management monetizes or rolls up the telecom assets intelligently, the discount can close quickly.

Bear target: $1,350, down 21% from $1,705. This assumes cash remains trapped, overhead persists, a discount to liquid assets returns, and no catalyst emerges.

Base target: $2,250, up 32%. This values net liquid assets plus the NH broadband business at a modest private-market multiple and Brick Skirt around carrying value, with continued buyback accretion.

Bull target: $2,900, up 70%. This assumes continued repurchases, 2026 operating EBITDA holding above the 2025 run rate, and either a strategic sale or a clearer holding-company value realization that removes the OTC cash-box discount.
